title: SplitTopologicalOrder

lean_statement.name: splitOrder
lean_statement.code:
/-- The *split-order* `splitOrder W r` on the carrier `α ⊕ ↑W`,
induced by a relation `r : α → α → Prop` and a set `W : Set α`.
This is the LN's interleaving recipe from claim_3_6: assign each
`v_j ∈ W` two new index slots `j − 1/3` (for `v_j^0 = Sum.inl v_j`)
and `j + 1/3` (for `v_j^1 = Sum.inr ⟨v_j, hv_j⟩`), then order by
index value. The four cases of the pattern match correspond to the
four `(Sum.inl/inr, Sum.inl/inr)` corner pairs; see the design block
above and the file-level docstring for the index-recipe derivation.

`noncomputable` because we do not assume any decidability on `r` or
`v ∈ W`; downstream uses are all `Prop`-valued so the classical
choice has no observable cost. Used by
`isTopologicalOrder_nodeSplittingOn` (this file) and quoted by name
in claim_3_9 (SWIG topological order) and the iSCM uniqueness
theory of chapters 8 -- 10. -/
noncomputable def splitOrder (W : Set α) (r : α → α → Prop) :
    (α ⊕ ↑W) → (α ⊕ ↑W) → Prop
  | Sum.inl v₁, Sum.inl v₂ => r v₁ v₂
  | Sum.inr ⟨w₁, _⟩, Sum.inr ⟨w₂, _⟩ => r w₁ w₂
  | Sum.inl v, Sum.inr ⟨w, _⟩ => r v w ∨ v = w
  | Sum.inr ⟨w, _⟩, Sum.inl v => r w v

lean_statement.name: isTopologicalOrder_nodeSplittingOn
lean_statement.code:
/-- claim_3_6 part A: if `W ⊆ G.V` and `r` is a topological order
of `G`, then `splitOrder W r` is a topological order of
`G.nodeSplittingOn W hW`. Mirrors the constructive half of the
`\Rem` immediately after def_3_11 in
`lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (lines 444 -- 455), with
the LN's `± 1/3` interleaving recipe encoded as the four-case
`splitOrder` pattern match.

The `W ⊆ G.V` precondition is structurally required by
`nodeSplittingOn` itself (def_3_11) -- the split edge
`Sum.inl w → Sum.inr ⟨w, hw⟩` needs its source `Sum.inl w` to live
in the split graph's output set, which is true only when
`w ∈ G.V`. This is not a topological-order condition; it is the
def_3_11 condition transported.

See `isAcyclic_nodeSplittingOn` below for the acyclicity half of
the LN remark and the file-level docstring for the rationale
behind splitting the LN's single `\Rem` into two theorems. -/
theorem isTopologicalOrder_nodeSplittingOn
    {G : CDMG α} {W : Set α} (hW : W ⊆ G.V)
    {r : α → α → Prop} (hr : G.IsTopologicalOrder r) :
    (G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).IsTopologicalOrder (splitOrder W r) := by

lean_statement.name: isAcyclic_nodeSplittingOn
lean_statement.code:
/-- claim_3_6 part B: if `W ⊆ G.V` and `G` is acyclic, then
`G.nodeSplittingOn W hW` is acyclic. Mirrors the acyclicity half
of the `\Rem` immediately after def_3_11 in
`lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (lines 444 -- 455); see
`isTopologicalOrder_nodeSplittingOn` above for the constructive
half and the file-level docstring for the rationale behind
splitting the LN's single `\Rem` into two theorems.

The `W ⊆ G.V` precondition is structurally required by
`nodeSplittingOn` itself (def_3_11), not by acyclicity per se;
see `isTopologicalOrder_nodeSplittingOn`. No `[Finite α]`
hypothesis is added at the statement phase to keep both proof
routes open (claim_3_2-based vs. direct walk-lifting); see the
per-theorem design block above for the discussion. -/
theorem isAcyclic_nodeSplittingOn
    {G : CDMG α} {W : Set α} (hW : W ⊆ G.V)
    (h : G.IsAcyclic) :
    (G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).IsAcyclic := by

lean_explanation:
This row formalises the LN's `\Rem` immediately after def 3.11 (the node-splitting $G_{\spl(W)}$) as three named objects: a noncomputable relation `splitOrder` plus two theorems showing that the LN's interleaving order is indeed a topological order and that acyclicity is preserved under node-splitting. The ambient `α : Type*` is the original vertex type, with no finiteness instance required. `W : Set α` is the subset on which to split, and `hW : W ⊆ G.V` is the precondition inherited from def 3.11 itself — the fresh split edge $v^0 \to v^1$ needs $v \in V$ for its source `Sum.inl v` to live in the split graph's output set, so every Lean mention of `nodeSplittingOn` carries this hypothesis. `r : α → α → Prop` is the original relation on $G$.

The split graph's carrier is the disjoint sum `α ⊕ ↑W` (Mathlib's `Sum`): the LN's `0`-copy $v_j^0$ is `Sum.inl v_j` and the `1`-copy $v_j^1$ is `Sum.inr ⟨v_j, hv_j⟩`, where `↑W` is the coercion of the set `W` to its subtype `{ x // x ∈ W }`. `splitOrder W r` lifts `r` to this carrier via a four-case pattern match on `Sum.inl` / `Sum.inr` that encodes the LN's "$j$ for $v_j$, $j - \tfrac{1}{3}$ for $v_j^0$, $j + \tfrac{1}{3}$ for $v_j^1$" recipe purely relationally — no real-valued index function is committed to, since the index recipe is only a *device* for visualising why the induced order works. Three of the four cases reduce to `r` on the underlying $\alpha$-elements; the fourth, `(Sum.inl v, Sum.inr ⟨w, _⟩)`, reads `r v w ∨ v = w`, with the `v = w` disjunct firing exactly on the LN's fresh split edge.

Part A (`isTopologicalOrder_nodeSplittingOn`) is the constructive half: from `hr : G.IsTopologicalOrder r` (the four-field bundle `irrefl` / `trans` / `trichotomous` / `parent_lt` from def 3.8) produce a topological order of `G.nodeSplittingOn W hW`, named explicitly as `splitOrder W r` so that downstream callers (the SWIG order of claim 3.9, the iSCM chapters' "order inherited from the split") can quote this specific construction by name. Part B (`isAcyclic_nodeSplittingOn`) is the side-condition half: from `h : G.IsAcyclic` conclude `(G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).IsAcyclic`. In both theorems `G`, `W`, and `r` are implicit (unifiable from `hW` / `hr` / the conclusion) while `hW` stays explicit because every mention of `nodeSplittingOn` must supply it.
design_choices:
**The LN's single `\Rem` is split into two named theorems.** Forré–Mooij bundle "$G_{\spl(W)}$ is acyclic" and "an explicit topological order on $G_{\spl(W)}$" under one paragraph. Lean splits them because the two halves have different downstream consumers. The topological-order half is the *named-construction* result — callers (claim 3.9's SWIG order, the iSCM chapters' "order inherited from the split") want `splitOrder W r` by name to plug into their own machinery. The acyclicity half is the *side-condition* result — callers (claim 3.7, claim 3.12, the iSCM well-foundedness theory) want `(G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).IsAcyclic` as a hypothesis without committing to a specific topological order. A bundled conjunction would force the second class either to project from a `∧` or to drag in topological-order data they do not need. The split mirrors claim 3.3's two-theorem decomposition for the analogous remark attached to `hardInterventionOn`.

**`splitOrder` is a standalone named definition with no `G` argument.** The relation is used in both halves of this row (part A directly; part B optionally via claim 3.2) and is itself the LN's named construction; factoring it out gives a single referent for "the topological order on the split graph from a topological order on $G$" so that downstream callers (claim 3.9's SWIG order; the iSCM chapters' "order inherited from the split") work with this exact relation by name rather than re-deriving the four-case match each time. The LN's hedge "*for instance*, a topological order for $G_{\spl(W)}$ can be achieved by ..." underlines that the $\pm \tfrac{1}{3}$ interleave is one concrete construction, not the canonical one: the theorem proves `splitOrder W r` is *a* topological order, not *the*, so the named-def form lets downstream uses pin down this specific recipe by name while remaining free to instantiate `IsTopologicalOrder` differently in contexts that favour a different interleaving. Defining the relation purely on the carrier `α ⊕ ↑W` (rather than as a method on `CDMG α`) also means it composes with arbitrary relations on $\alpha$; the `G.IsTopologicalOrder r` hypothesis enters only when the theorem `isTopologicalOrder_nodeSplittingOn` asks `(G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).IsTopologicalOrder (splitOrder W r)`.

**Sum-shaped pattern match, with an asymmetric `r v w ∨ v = w` disjunct on the `(Sum.inl, Sum.inr)` case.** The carrier `α ⊕ ↑W` encodes the `0`-copy / `1`-copy distinction at the type level via `Sum.inl` / `Sum.inr`, so a four-case pattern match on the pair is the natural shape; a `dite (v ∈ W)` approach would force every case to use classical decidability of set membership and lose the structural recursion `Sum.casesOn` provides for free. The disjunct `v = w` is the LN's "split edge" condition: when $v = w \in W$ the split graph adds a fresh directed edge $v^0 \to v^1$, and `parent_lt` on the topological-order proof demands this edge be respected. Encoding the equality as a disjunct (rather than as a side condition or a separate edge case) keeps the construction first-order and lets `parent_lt` discharge the split-edge case via `Or.inr rfl`. The `(Sum.inr, Sum.inl)` case carries no analogous disjunct: that asymmetry reflects the directed nature of the split-edge convention $w^0 \to w^1$ — the $\pm \tfrac{1}{3}$ index shift makes `(Sum.inl w, Sum.inr w)` an edge but the reverse pair not an edge, so the corresponding direction in `splitOrder` collapses to plain `r w v` with no equality branch.

**`splitOrder` is `noncomputable`, and neither theorem requires `[Finite α]`.** The relation `r` is `Prop`-valued and need not be decidable, so `splitOrder`'s case-analysis on the disjunction `r v w ∨ v = w` cannot be made computable in general; marking the def `noncomputable` keeps it consistent with `nodeSplittingOn`'s own classical declaration, and downstream uses (membership in `IsTopologicalOrder`) are all `Prop`-valued so the choice has no observable cost. Part A's proof transports the four `IsTopologicalOrder` fields via the `@[simp]` membership lemmas of the node-splitting construction, which never enumerate vertices. Part B's statement leaves two proof routes open: route (i) via claim 3.2's `isAcyclic_iff_hasTopologicalOrder` composed with part A (which pulls in `[Finite α]` for the $\to$ direction of claim 3.2), or route (ii) a direct walk-lifting argument analogous to claim 3.3 part A's projection through the split-graph carrier (no finiteness needed). Keeping `[Finite α]` off the hypothesis list at the statement phase lets downstream callers in iSCM chapters apply both results over not-yet-finitised vertex types; if the direct route turns out to be infeasible the prover can add `[Finite α]` later without breaking statement-level consumers.

**Binder choice and naming convention.** `G`, `W`, and `r` are implicit because they are unifiable from `hW : W ⊆ G.V`, `hr : G.IsTopologicalOrder r`, and the conclusion. `hW` is explicit because the LN takes $W \subseteq V$ for granted at def 3.11, but in Lean the precondition is part of `nodeSplittingOn`'s own signature and must be supplied every time the construction is mentioned. The names follow Mathlib's `<conclusion>_<construction>` convention — `isTopologicalOrder_nodeSplittingOn` reads as "the `IsTopologicalOrder` predicate applied to the `nodeSplittingOn` construction" — and match the precedent set by `isTopologicalOrder_hardInterventionOn` and `isAcyclic_hardInterventionOn` from claim 3.3.
